did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used the open-source AI algorithm 'All Data Are Ext' (ADAE) to predict melanoma from dermoscopy skin lesion images. The study prospectively validated the diagnostic accuracy of this AI algorithm and assessed its impact on dermatologist decision-making for melanoma diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is titled 'Prospective validation of dermoscopy-based open-source artificial intelligence for melanoma diagnosis (PROVE-AI study)' and explicitly states it conducted a 'prospective, observational clinical study' to validate the accuracy of an AI algorithm (ADAE) and assess its impact on dermatologist decision-making. It reports original experimental results including participant enrollment (435 participants, 603 lesions), diagnostic metrics (sensitivity, specificity, AUC), and statistical analyses, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Prospective validation of dermoscopy-based open-source artificial intelligence for melanoma diagnosis (PROVE-AI study)
brief_summary: This prospective, observational clinical study validated the diagnostic accuracy of the open-source AI algorithm 'All Data Are Ext' (ADAE) for melanoma detection using dermoscopy images. The study enrolled 435 participants contributing 603 lesions, with 95 confirmed melanomas, to assess ADAE's sensitivity at a predefined 95% threshold. ADAE achieved a sensitivity of 96.8% and a specificity of 37.4% at this threshold, demonstrating performance superior to dermatologists' pre-AI assessments. Exposure to ADAE scores significantly improved dermatologists' ability to assess melanoma risk, increasing their AUC from 0.7798 to 0.8161 (p = 0.042). Post-ADAE management decisions showed equivalent or higher net benefit compared to biopsying all lesions, suggesting theoretical potential to improve clinical workflows. The authors conclude that larger randomized trials are needed to fully evaluate the adoption of this AI algorithm in clinical practice.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target of the study; 95 confirmed melanomas (49 in situ, 46 invasive) out of 603 total lesions.
Most common non-melanoma class; 312 nevi out of 603 total lesions.
Non-melanoma class; 28 atypical melanocytic proliferations reported.
Non-melanoma class; 22 keratinocyte carcinomas reported. Specific subtypes mentioned in context of scores include basal cell carcinoma and SCC in situ.
Non-melanoma class; mentioned in context of ADAE score distribution and specificity analysis.
Non-melanoma class; mentioned in context of ADAE score distribution and specificity analysis.
Descriptive category for 11 lesions (1.8%) that lacked pigment; included 1 melanoma, 1 atypical melanocytic proliferation, 1 SCC in situ, and 8 benign lesions.

all_task_types.details: The AI algorithm ADAE predicts the probability of melanoma from dermoscopy images to classify lesions as melanoma or non-melanoma. The study validates diagnostic accuracy (sensitivity, specificity, AUC) for this binary classification task. The model outputs a score representing the likelihood of melanoma, which is used to determine a binary management decision (biopsy vs. no biopsy) based on a predefined sensitivity threshold.

datasets_used.name: Prospective Clinical Cohort (PROVE-AI Study)
datasets_used.description: A prospective, observational clinical cohort of patients enrolled from dermatology clinics in New York and New Jersey (Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center) between September 2021 and June 2022. Participants were adults (≥18 years) who consented to a skin biopsy to exclude melanoma. Lesions were excluded if previously biopsied, recurrent, mucosal, or removed for cosmetic purposes.
classes_used: Keratinocyte Carcinoma (SCC in situ, Basal Cell Carcinoma)

use.testing: Used as the primary test set to validate the diagnostic accuracy (sensitivity, specificity, AUC) of the ADAE algorithm in a prospective clinical setting.
use.other: Images from this cohort were made freely available through the International Skin Imaging Collaboration’s online image archive for external analyses and benchmarking.

datasets_used.name: Retrospective Non-Enrolled Cohort
datasets_used.description: A retrospective dataset of skin lesions that underwent biopsy during the study period but were not enrolled in the prospective study. These lesions had a 'rule out' clinical diagnosis including a melanocytic diagnostic term on the pathology requisition order. Limited to dermatologists who consented ≥20 participants to the prospective study.

use.testing: Used to assess potential recruitment bias by comparing ADAE performance on non-enrolled lesions against the prospective cohort.

datasets_used.name: Retrospective MSK High-Risk Screening Cohort (2020)
datasets_used.description: A retrospective dataset of 147 consecutive biopsies (46 melanomas and 101 non-melanomas) ruled out for melanoma performed in 2020 from a high-risk screening clinic at Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center.

use.testing: Used to validate the temporal validity of the ADAE prediction model prior to the initiation of the prospective study.

datasets_used.name: SIIM-ISIC 2020 Melanoma Classification Challenge Test Set
datasets_used.description: A convenience test set of 10,982 public dermoscopy images from 6 dermatology centers (Barcelona, Spain; New York, United States; Vienna, Austria; Sydney, Australia; Brisbane, Australia; Athens, Greece) used for the 2020 challenge. ADAE was the top-ranked algorithm on this set.

use.testing: Used to define the predetermined threshold (95% sensitivity) for the prospective study and to benchmark ADAE's original performance (AUC 0.9490).
use.other: Used as a reference distribution for ADAE scores in the web-app interface visualization.

datasets_used.name: SIIM-ISIC 2019 Melanoma Classification Challenge Test Set
datasets_used.description: The test set from the 2019 SIIM-ISIC challenge, used for retrospective performance comparison.

use.testing: Used to validate ADAE's performance relative to the top-ranked algorithm from the 2019 challenge (ADAE AUC: 0.970 vs. 0.949).

datasets_used.name: SIIM-ISIC 2018 Melanoma Classification Challenge Test Set
datasets_used.description: The test set from the 2018 SIIM-ISIC challenge, used for retrospective performance comparison.

use.testing: Used to validate ADAE's performance relative to the top-ranked algorithm from the 2018 challenge (ADAE AUC: 0.932 vs. 0.928).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To estimate the uncertainty around the sensitivity and specificity of the ADAE algorithm at the predetermined threshold.

statistical_methods_used.method: Two-proportion z-test (implied by p-values for sensitivity/specificity comparisons)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the sensitivity of ADAE for invasive vs. in situ melanoma, and to compare ADAE performance on the prospective cohort vs. the retrospective non-enrolled cohort.
To compare the Area Under the Curve (AUC) of the ADAE algorithm against the dermatologists' pre-AI assessments and to compare raw vs. log-transformed output aggregation AUCs.
To identify factors associated with the specificity of the ADAE algorithm.
To assess the clinical utility of post-ADAE dermatologist decisions compared to the baseline strategy of biopsying all lesions.
To assess changes in individual dermatologists' confidence in their diagnosis after exposure to ADAE scores.

statistical_methods_used.method: Wilcoxon signed-rank test (implied by IQR comparisons and p < 0.001 for calibration)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the change in mean calibration of dermatologist predicted melanoma probability after ADAE exposure.

hypothesis_testing.test: DeLong's test for correlated ROC curves; Fisher's exact test; Binomial generalized linear models (GLM) with cluster-robust standard errors; Kruskal-Wallis tests; Two-proportion z-test (implied by p-values for sensitivity comparisons); Wilcoxon signed-rank test (implied by calibration p-value)
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in the Area Under the Curve (AUC) between the dermatologists' pre-AI assessments and post-ADAE assessments; there is no association between categorical factors and binary outcomes; there is no difference in ADAE score distributions between subgroups.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a difference in the AUC between pre-AI and post-ADAE dermatologist assessments; there is an association between categorical factors and binary outcomes; there are differences in ADAE score distributions between subgroups.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The study reported that the dermatologists' ability to assess melanoma risk significantly improved after ADAE exposure (AUC 0.7798 vs. 0.8161, p = 0.042), indicating statistical significance at the alpha = 0.05 level. Other reported p-values (e.g., p = 0.609 for sensitivity difference, p < 0.001 for calibration change) were used to determine statistical significance or lack thereof for specific comparisons.

Prospective Clinical Cohort (PROVE-AI St.notes: The difference in ADAE sensitivity for invasive vs. in situ melanoma was not significant (p = 0.609). Specificity was significantly associated with patient age, anatomic site, and maximum diameter. Specificity was higher in patients with skin-type III than skin-type I (p = 0.002). ADAE score distribution varied significantly by dermatologist (p < 0.001). For the 5 dominant dermatologists, ADAE sensitivity at the 95% threshold ranged from 89% to 100%, and specificity ranged from 0% to 100%.

Retrospective Non-Enrolled Cohort.notes: Used to assess recruitment bias. No significant difference in diagnostic accuracy estimates was identified compared to the prospective cohort.

Retrospective MSK High-Risk Screening Co.notes: Used to validate temporal validity of the prediction model prior to the prospective study.

SIIM-ISIC 2020 Melanoma Classification C.notes: ADAE was the top-ranked algorithm. The difference between raw and log-transformed aggregation AUCs was not significant (p = 0.4177; DeLong’s test). This set was used to define the 95% sensitivity threshold for the prospective study.

SIIM-ISIC 2019 Melanoma Classification C.notes: ADAE exceeded the performance of the top-ranked algorithm from the 2019 challenge.

SIIM-ISIC 2018 Melanoma Classification C.notes: ADAE exceeded the performance of the top-ranked algorithm from the 2018 challenge.

architecture_used.pretraining: Trained exclusively on public dermoscopy images from the SIIM-ISIC Melanoma Classification Challenge datasets.
architecture_used.input_size: Dermoscopy skin lesion images; image size used as clinical metadata input.

architecture_details.backbone: 16 models based on EfficientNet architecture; 2 models based on ResNet architecture
architecture_details.folds: Each of the 18 models trained on 5 different folds, resulting in a total of 90 model weight files
architecture_details.metadata_input: Four models incorporate clinical metadata (age, sex, anatomic site, and image size)
architecture_details.ensemble_method: Post-softmax values for 4 rotation and 2 flipping states are averaged per model. The 90 individual fold scores are log-transformed prior to averaging to produce the final ADAE score.

training_details.dataset: SIIM-ISIC 2020 Melanoma Classification Challenge test set (10,982 public dermoscopy images)

classification_or_output_head.thresholding: Binary classification based on a predetermined threshold corresponding to 95% sensitivity on the multi-institutional test set

preprocessing_techniques: Image upload via web-app interface for real-time processing
Post-softmax averaging of 4 rotation and 2 flipping states per model
Log-transformation of 90 individual fold scores prior to averaging to produce final ADAE score
Spline transformation of scores to display 95% sensitivity threshold in web-app interface
Generation of saliency maps to show spatial support for melanoma prediction

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Post-softmax values were averaged across 4 rotation states per model during inference/ensembling.
Post-softmax values were averaged across 2 flipping states per model during inference/ensembling.

conclusions: The open-source AI algorithm ADAE demonstrated high diagnostic accuracy for melanoma in a prospective clinical setting, achieving a sensitivity of 96.8% (95% CI: 91.1–98.9%) and specificity of 37.4% (95% CI: 33.3–41.7%) at the predetermined 95% sensitivity threshold.
Exposure to ADAE scores significantly improved dermatologists' ability to assess melanoma risk, increasing their AUC from 0.7798 to 0.8161 (p = 0.042).

Post-ADAE dermatologist management decisions showed equivalent or higher net benefit compared to the baseline strategy of biopsying all lesions, suggesting theoretical potential to improve clinical workflows by reducing unnecessary biopsies.

ADAE specificity was significantly associated with patient age, anatomic site, and maximum diameter, with lower specificity observed in patients aged 65+, lesions on the head/neck, and lesions larger than 6 mm.
Specificity was higher in patients with Fitzpatrick skin type III compared to skin type I (p = 0.002).

The study acknowledges limitations including a relatively small sample size, lack of diversity in the cohort (96% White participants), and the absence of patients with Fitzpatrick Skin Types V or VI, which affects generalizability.

Larger randomized trials and multicenter studies are needed to fully evaluate the potential of adopting this AI algorithm into clinical workflows and to validate its transportability across diverse populations.
important_remarks: The study population lacked diversity, with 96% of participants being White individuals and no patients with Fitzpatrick Skin Types V or VI included, limiting generalizability to darker skin tones.
The study was conducted at a single center (Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center), which limits the external validity and transportability of the findings to other clinical settings.
The sample size was relatively small (435 participants, 603 lesions), and the study acknowledged being underpowered for subgroup comparisons, particularly for rare melanoma subtypes.

Not all eligible participants were approached for inclusion due to time, funding, and staff constraints, which may have introduced recruitment bias, although retrospective analysis of non-enrolled lesions showed no significant difference in diagnostic accuracy.
The study did not assess non-biopsied skin lesions or biopsied melanomas not considered potential melanomas, restricting the evaluation to a specific clinical workflow (rule-out melanoma).

ADAE specificity varied significantly by dermatologist (ranging from 0% to 100% for the 5 dominant dermatologists), indicating that the algorithm's performance is influenced by the specific clinical context and image characteristics presented by different providers.

The algorithm's specificity was significantly lower in patients aged 65+, on the head/neck, and for lesions larger than 6 mm, suggesting potential performance disparities based on patient and lesion characteristics.

While the study demonstrated theoretical potential for improving clinical workflows via decision curve analysis, it did not conduct a randomized controlled trial, and the authors explicitly state that larger randomized trials are needed to fully evaluate adoption.
